2.Reverse Causality Problem: IsTcausingY, or isYcausingT?
Example 1: Is \college" causing a person to have a higher socioeconomic status,
or is having a high socioeconomic status causing an increase on the chances that a
person attends college?
Example 2: What is the e
ect of the number of police ocers on crime?
Hypothesis: Having more police ocers will lead to lower crime rates.
One may estimate an OLS of the number of crimes on the number of police ocers
by, e.g., zip codes or cities adjusting for factors such as income , race, unemployment
rate, etc. We usually

nd:
corr(police;crime )>0
Some may conclude that police ocers cause more crime, but this is just correlation!
Why is this happening? It is actually high crime rates that cause cities to hire more
police ocers (Reverse Causality)!
Hence, we need to ask: Does TcauseY, or doesYcauseT?
=)In sum, we cannot rely on OLS to \magically" solve our causality problems. Then,
what do we do?

Now, most governments and non-government organizations (e.g. World Bank) require

1.Correlation6=Causation

nd that

chunks.section: Corr (A;B)>0 (or<0). This could be due to
chunks.text:
Corr (A;B)>0 (or<0). This could be due to:
(a)A!B (what we want)
(b)B!A (reverse causality)
(c)C!A&B (confounding)
No way to tell without a clear research design!
8

We ask : under what assumptions can we jump from correlation to causation?
Avoid statements like those we typically hear on TV or radio: \A recent paper found
that watching TV causes obesity". Chances are, the paper found association, not
causation! E.g., if that statement is true, then the implication is that we should ban
TVs from all the gyms because they cause obesity (?). Of course not!
This is a trivial example, but unfortunately, that mistake is more common than
you can imagine at all levels (industry, government, academia, etc.), and for very
important topics!
2. No \Free Lunch" in Econometrics. Every research design/identi
cation strategy has
its pros and cons, even an experiment!

Cons:
Usually very costly
Time consuming (e.g. for \college" example, we would need to wait many years
to see outcomes; or the urgent need for a coronavirus vaccine).
Ethical issues
Individuals/\clones" may not do what we ask them to (e.g. some in control
group may attend college anyways)
Avoid saying: \This approach is always perfect/adequate".

Final Note:
It is dangerous to jump from correlation to causation (see examples above, such as
the police-crime or TV-obesity ones).
But: It may also be risky to ignore all associations/correlations: causality may
be hidden in some, and it is not always easy to come up with good research designs
or perform an experiment.

1 Introduction
In these notes, we de
ne what a causal e
ect is, and what our causal parameters of interest
will be (i.e., what we want to estimate). We also discuss the main challenges to estimate

The second key element of the Potential Outcome Framework is the Assignment Mech-
anism, which answers the question : how whether individual igetsTi= 0 or Ti= 1 is

The Assignment Mechanism formalizes how each unit gets either Ti= 1 or Ti= 0.
{Examples: how is \college attendance" assigned? How is \food stamps" as-
signed? How is \Medicaid" assigned?
The Assignment Mechanism plays a key role when estimating causal e
ects: What
research design or tool we use depends on how Twas assigned. Examples:
{IfTis assigned at random (e.g., by a lottery) = )use experimental design.
{IfTis assigned based on variables that we observe (e.g., age, SAT scores, income,
etc.) =)use a \selection on observables" approach (e.g. OLS, matching).
{IfTis assigned based on variables that are unobserved to us (e.g., ability,
motivation, etc.) = )use a \selection on unobservables" approach (e.g., IV).
Economic Theory and Institutional Knowledge play a key role in understanding how
Tis assigned.

5 Drawbacks/Limitations of Experiments
As we have discussed, every approach in econometrics has pros and cons, even randomized

experiments. Some drawbacks/limitations of Experimental Designs are:
Some treatments are dicult to randomize (e.g., \attending college").
Expensive and can take a lot of time.
There may be ethical issues.
Results apply only to a particular population and time.
{For example, results from an experiment to assess the e
ects of a training pro-
gram in California in the 1980's may not be informative for training programs
in other states (e.g., Florida) today.

Other usual problems in practice:
{Attrition: some people are \missing" in follow-up surveys or interviews, leading
to missing outcomes.
It can cause issues if, as is usually the case, the outcomes are not \missing
at random". E.g., suppose we only have outcomes for \rich" people, but
not for \poor" people. That may bias our results.
{Non-compliance: people may not comply with their assignment (also called
\cross-over").
For example, some units assigned to enroll in a program do notenroll, and
some units assigned not to enroll manage to enroll.

experiments. Some drawbacks/limitations of Experimental Designs are:
Some treatments are dicult to randomize (e.g., \attending college").
Expensive and can take a lot of time.
There may be ethical issues.
Results apply only to a particular population and time.
{For example, results from an experiment to assess the e
ects of a training pro-
gram in California in the 1980's may not be informative for training programs
in other states (e.g., Florida) today.

Other usual problems in practice:
{Attrition: some people are \missing" in follow-up surveys or interviews, leading
to missing outcomes.
It can cause issues if, as is usually the case, the outcomes are not \missing
at random". E.g., suppose we only have outcomes for \rich" people, but
not for \poor" people. That may bias our results.
{Non-compliance: people may not comply with their assignment (also called
\cross-over").
For example, some units assigned to enroll in a program do notenroll, and
some units assigned not to enroll manage to enroll.

As we have discussed, every approach in econometrics has pros and cons, even randomized

experiments. Some drawbacks/limitations of Experimental Designs are:
Some treatments are dicult to randomize (e.g., \attending college").
Expensive and can take a lot of time.
There may be ethical issues.
Results apply only to a particular population and time.
{For example, results from an experiment to assess the e
ects of a training pro-
gram in California in the 1980's may not be informative for training programs
in other states (e.g., Florida) today.

Other usual problems in practice:
{Attrition: some people are \missing" in follow-up surveys or interviews, leading
to missing outcomes.
It can cause issues if, as is usually the case, the outcomes are not \missing
at random". E.g., suppose we only have outcomes for \rich" people, but
not for \poor" people. That may bias our results.
{Non-compliance: people may not comply with their assignment (also called
\cross-over").
For example, some units assigned to enroll in a program do notenroll, and
some units assigned not to enroll manage to enroll.

1 Introduction
Although experimental designs and random assignment are the gold-standard for estimat-
ing causal e
ects, we are not always able to estimate e

non-experimental designs.
Unconfoundedness-Based Designs are likely the most widely used methods in practice (for
good or bad), as they do not require an Instrument or a Quasi-Experiment , which can be
hard to
nd. However, it is important to understand their assumptions and limitations.

Assumption 2 (Overlap). For all possible values of the covariates X, there is a positive

1 Introduction
In these notes, we discuss estimation of the ATE and theATT in an unconfoundedness-
based design. In other words, we ask, how can we estimate the ATE and theATT in this
design. We start by discussing estimation of the ATE , and later we discuss estimation of
theATT .
Remember that the two assumptions on which unconfoundedness-based designs are based
are Unconfoundedness (Assumption 1) and Overlap (Assumption 2). As discussed in detail
in the previous notes, under these two assumptions we can identify the ATE (i.e., write it

chunks.section: as a function of observed variables) as
chunks.text:
as a function of observed variables) as:
ATE =EX[E[YjT= 1;X=x]�E[YjT= 0;X=x]] (1)
If we only have onebinaryX(e.g., gender), then it is easy to estimate the ATE based on
equation (1) by using an approach similar to what we did for an experimental design for
each of the values of X(see last lecture).
Example. Suppose unconfoundedness holds with one covariate, gender, with X= 1 if the
individual is female, and X= 0 if the individual is male. Under unconfoundedness,
it is \as if" we have an experiment within the X= 1 group (females) and the X= 0
group (males).
Hence, within the X=xgroup we can identify the ATE for that group as:
ATE (x) =E[YjT= 1;X=x]| {z }

1 Advanced Econometrics Prof. Carlos A. Flores Economics 440 Cal Poly State University Problem Set 1: Randomized Experiments Instructions: To answer the questions in this problem set, please do not just attach the program output and expect me to find the answers there. Answers should be clearly stated or will receive zero points. Preferably, turn in your answers separately from the program output. In any case, all the code and the program output should be attached as an appendix to your problem set. Problem sets without code will receive a score of zero. Also, no late submission of the problem set will be accepted. Setting: You recently got an internship at the Manpower Demonstration Research Corporation (MDRC), which is a “nonprofit, nonpartisan education and social policy research organization dedicated to learning what works to improve programs and policies that affect the poor,” as stated in its website.1 Jerzy Rubin, your supervisor and an expert on causal inference, always complains to his colleague Donald Neyman that the only thing his economics and other “analytics” interns know how to do is run regressions (or machine learning methods), without ever thinking hard about what they are really doing or if the effects they estimate are really causal. To prove his point, he typically gives his interns one of their classical social randomized experiments on evaluation of training programs to see what they do with it (“besides running regressions”, he usually says). He does the same with you this time, and asks you to write a one-page report on your findings. Of course, he does not know you took ECON 440 at Cal Poly J. For the purpose of this problem set, do not write the one-page report (I’ll probably ask that next year J). The questions below would be part of the main analysis you would perform for such report. Problem: You are asked to evaluate the effects on earnings of the National Supported Work Demonstration (NSW) job training program, whose main objective was to help disadvantaged workers improve their labor market prospects by providing them with work experience and counseling.2 During the mid-1970s, the MDRC performed a study on the effects of the NSW program. In it, individuals were randomly assigned to either receive or not job training. Baseline earnings were measured in 1975, which was the year before they were assigned to either the treatment or control group. Earnings were subsequently measured in 1978, a few years after the treated individuals had gone through the NSW program (the program was about 12 to 18 months long). The program focused on four groups: women on Aid to Families with Dependent Children (AFDC) for many years, ex-criminal offenders, ex-drug addicts, and high school dropout (for the last three groups, both men and women were considered). Data: The data set you will use is called nsw_econ440.dta (available on Canvas).
